The New York-based company has chosen superconducting qubits as the basis for its quantum computers. In November, IBM announced that it is making quantum processors at NY Creates’ Albany NanoTech Complex in New York.

“Superconducting qubits are one of the newer modalities,” Crowder said. “It’s been around for a little under 20 years, not as long as photonics or trapped ions. We believe it has the right balance of speed and quality and programmability. You need all three of those to build a quantum computer. Superconducting qubits are about a thousand times raw operation speed faster than trapped ions, which are probably the number-two modality right now.”

Modular fault-torrent quantum computers need efficient error-correction codes and connectivity between qubits that are not just next-nearest neighbors.

“There’s some complexity in terms of having some long-range connections between your qubits,” Crowder said. “From a semiconductor manufacturing point of view, what that implies is that not only do you have to have good quality, high-coherence qubit fabrication processes, but you also need to have advanced-packaging capabilities to do the multi-level packaging and those long-range connections in a way that’s very high fidelity.”

IBM will never exit semiconductors from an R&D perspective, Crowder added.

“We’re able to leverage that and leverage our involvement up in Albany to have access to a 300-millimeter fab to do this. That has had very significant advantages for us, which are going to get more significant going forward.”

The roadmap

IBM plans to build and demonstrate fault-tolerant, error-correcting quantum computing by 2028, and expects to release its related Starling quantum computer to customers in 2029. The company also plans to achieve milestones codenamed as birds before that time.

IBM’s Loon, sometime this year, will be the first demonstration of a packaged chip that has the long-range connections, allowing the creation of a quantum memory and testing of coding software.

Announced in November, IBM’s Nighthawk quantum processor is laid out in a square lattice, which means it has more next-nearest neighbors, potentially allowing a program to run with 30% fewer operations than the previous generation.

“It’s going to be really important for that short-term provable quantum advantage milestone,” Crowder said. “It’s going to allow you to more effectively use the 5,000, 10,000, 15,000 operations that you can do today before you get to fault tolerance. It’s the next-generation processor in our short-term roadmap, which allows the first demonstrations of advantage in the next 12 months.”

Crowder outlines a series of following steps toward Starling, a full 200-qubit hundred-million-plus operations quantum computer that IBM is going to build in Poughkeepsie, N.Y.

“You need a logical processing unit that injects your actual instructions into your quantum memory,” Crowder notes. “You need something called a magic-state factory to create non-Clifford gates so you have a universal gate set. If you’re going to build it in a modular way, which I think any system is going to have to do, you need an adapter to allow you to connect the packages together and have entanglement across those packages.”

Quantum customers

IBM’s quantum customers include Lockheed in propulsion chemistry; Boeing in anti-corrosion, anti-degradation chemistry; Cleveland Clinic in chemistry-of-life simulations; and the U.S. Department of Energy “looking at a whole bunch of chemistry-related things that tie in with their mission,” Crowder said.

IBM has more than 100 groups in its network, including industry clients, academic and research clients, and commercial partners.

The company has seven quantum systems deployed in external locations, in addition to IBM’s systems in data centers that are part of the company’s shared cloud service.

IBM has deployed quantum systems in Japan, Korea, Spain, the U.S., and near an IBM facility in Bromont, Quebec.

“We know how to put systems in other people’s data centers,” Crowder said. “We do the hardware refresh every two years. Right now, the model works better if it’s IBM owned and operated. In the future, if they wanted to buy it as hardware plus software as opposed to a service, we’re open to that. Right now, it’s just easier for everybody to do it the way we’re doing it.”

data.title: After Nexperia: Can Europe Protect Chips Without Scaring Off Investors?
data.description:
//php echo do_shortcode('[responsivevoice_button voice="US English Male" buttontext="Listen to Post"]') ?>

The Dutch government’s emergency intervention of Nexperia—widely interpreted as an unprecedented assertion of state authority over a foreign-owned, strategically sensitive chip firm—has triggered a new debate across the EU. How far should governments go to protect semiconductor assets? And can Europe defend its sovereignty without deterring the very investment that keeps its chip ecosystem competitive?

Exclusive interviews with two experts closely following the evolution of Europe’s strategic safeguards show that the Nexperia incident is not an isolated shock, but a visible milestone in a continent-wide shift. Floor Doppen, senior program associate at the CELIS Institute, and Nick Houttekier, researcher at the Royal Military Academy in Belgium, describe a landscape where screening tools, industrial policy, geopolitics and state-firm relations are interacting in new and unpredictable ways.

For Houttekier, the Nexperia case is “clearly part of a broader shift” rather than a one-off reaction. Europe, he argues, has been drifting for several years into a geo-economic turn, a world where trade and investment policy are intertwined with security concerns and geopolitical rivalry.

According to Houttekier, the shift began around 2020 and has only accelerated since the pandemic and the deepening of U.S.–China competition.

 “Instruments with a specific geo-economic goal are growing in number, changing the relation between states and firms,” he said. “Closer firm-state relations are resulting from the growing number of instruments that either provide incentives in the form of industrial policy or restrict the freedom of movement of firms or other private entities.”

Nexperia, he noted, crystallizes that evolution. The U.S. had already placed the parent company Wingtech on the Entity List in December 2024. Then on, Sept. 29, 2025, the U.S. Bureau of Industry and Security issued a rule extending U.S. export control restrictions to entities at least 50% owned by one or more entities on the U.S. entity list, which created downstream risks for Nexperia’s operations. That external shock forced The Hague to assume “direct operational control,” revealing how deeply Europe is now embedded in the U.S.-China rivalry.

Differences in cultures and the informal cooperation that stitches them together

Doppen’s research on investment screening shows that EU member states traditionally differed widely in how closely they work with domestic industry. France historically maintains tight informal coordination with key firms, while the Netherlands and the U.K. keep more distance. And Germany is somewhere in between.

“I see a strong informal relationship emerging between governments and semiconductor firms across Europe,” she said. “Geopolitics is pushing firms to engage with governments for insights and guidance.”

Still, cultural differences matter. France, Doppen observed, is accustomed to intervention. The Dutch model, built on openness and arms-length interactions between the government and companies, made the Nexperia takeover feel like an anomaly, even if it fit the new geo-economic era. Italy, she added, shares more of France’s willingness to intervene directly when strategic sectors are at stake.

This divergence will matter as Europe tries to develop a coordinated semiconductor-security framework. While the European Commission is pushing for harmonization, member states retain final authority for national-security decisions, and their approaches reflect deep institutional cultures.

Foreign direct investment (FDI) screening—now present in almost every EU country—was meant to give governments a structured way to review foreign acquisitions. But semiconductor security cannot rely on screening alone. For Doppen, investment screening can be “too slow and too rigid” in high-innovation sectors. Technologies evolve faster than the frameworks designed to review them.

“It protects, but it does not necessarily offer the flexibility needed for rapid intervention,” she said, adding that she expects the next wave of tools to involve security valves, such as broader ex-post powers or targeted emergency mechanisms.

Greenfield investments, such as new fabs and R&D centers, illustrate the gap. Most national screening laws do not cover greenfield projects, even though a new foreign-built fab could become strategically sensitive later. The Netherlands and Germany do not review greenfield investments at all, while Sweden and a few others do.

According to Houttekier, because many European countries did not introduce screening regimes until 2023, many critical assets were acquired long before screening existed. “You cannot go back without expropriation,” he said. “FDI screening will help in the future, but it cannot solve legacy ownership issues.”

And this is precisely what made Nexperia so politically explosive: a strategically important chip factory already owned by a Chinese parent company, with no clear path to rebalancing operational control—except through emergency takeover.

Several member states are now exploring mechanisms that go beyond review and into outright governance. France already uses golden shares and emergency nationalizations—tools that allow temporary control over firms without permanently disrupting investor rights. The French population, Doppen said, is less alarmed by direct state involvement. In past cases, French officials simply made clear they would block a transaction if food or energy security was at stake.

“They would shrug it off,” she said. “Intervention is part of the model.”

Houttekier said that these powers should remain reserved for “the most acute threats,” but acknowledges they will become more frequent. As industrial policy expands, such as subsidies under the EU Chips Act, states naturally attach conditions, including governance rights, supply-chain disclosure and restrictions on foreign ownership. Those requirements, he warned, may quietly disadvantage foreign-owned European semiconductor companies, not through spectacular takeovers but through reduced access to funding and R&D programs.

One of Doppen’s most important findings concerns the informal cooperation between member states and the European Commission. Even though screening decisions remain national, governments regularly exchange risk assessments and political signals behind the scenes.

“There is a lot of informal communication,” she said. “Especially when there is alignment on the security threat.”

This informal layer may be crucial for semiconductors, where risks cross borders and supply chains span multiple EU jurisdictions. Yet it raises its own problems: mitigation agreements—restrictions imposed on foreign investors—are often opaque even between member states.

Doppen points out that member states cannot easily take security concerns of another state into account in their screening decisions or their mitigation agreements. A remedy agreed in a transaction may include the protection of data if stored abroad, but only the data of that particular state. For example, Spain may include a provision that requires the investor to protect Spanish data, when stored abroad, but may not be able to extend this provision to German or French data.

Another example of the complexities she gives is that a small supplier in the Netherlands might be critical to a French defense contractor, yet Dutch law may not allow intervention on behalf of foreign security concerns. The result is a patchwork that risks inefficiency, over-compliance and gaps in actual security.

What Europe should do next

According to Houttekier, Europe should avoid focusing only on leading-edge technology, such as 3-nm nodes or EUV lithography. The U.S.–China rivalry has shifted from cutting-edge chips to mature nodes and could shift again.

“It’s like a waterbed,” he said. “Push in one place, the pressure moves somewhere else.”

Europe must also map its exposure across the entire value chain—materials, equipment, packaging, and crucially, dependencies that are hard to substitute. Blind spots can appear in unexpected segments, from raw materials to legacy chips to packaging tools.

All European countries face the same fundamental dilemma: How to protect sovereignty without undermining the investment that sustains innovation. Doppen stresses that this tension is not new but is now becoming more acute.

“Regulation and attracting FDI have always been in conflict,” she said. “The geopolitical context just amplifies the stakes.”

For Houttekier, there is an additional risk: overcorrecting for security threats and missing the systemic forces that shape global power. Europe’s strength, he said, does not come from dominating one choke point—ASML’s EUV machines, SWIFT, or rare earth alternatives—but from the totality of its industrial base, alliances, financial systems and security cooperation. Fixating too narrowly on one risk creates new vulnerabilities elsewhere.

The Nexperia takeover was dramatic, but it was also symbolic—a sign that Europe’s semiconductor-security regime is moving beyond notification requirements toward operational intervention powers. What remains uncertain is how far member states are willing to go, and whether Europe can coordinate these tools without fracturing the single market or scaring away essential investment.

The only certainty is that Europe’s playbook for chip security is expanding—and the semiconductor industry must adapt to a world where policy, geopolitics and technology are now inseparable.

data.title: India’s Yotta Plans U.S. Listing to Finance India’s Rising AI Compute Demand
data.description:
//php echo do_shortcode('[responsivevoice_button voice="US English Male" buttontext="Listen to Post"]') ?>

Indian data-center operator Yotta Data Services’ parent company, Nidar Infrastructure, recently announced a plan to list on the NASDAQ through a merger with Cartica Acquisition Corp., a U.S.-based special purpose acquisition company (SPAC). The deal values Nidar at about $2.75 billion pre-transaction, according to filings.

Cartica’s Form F-4 registration statement for the transaction has been declared effective by the U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC). Following shareholder approval, the combined company will list on the NASDAQ Global Market under the tickers YTTA (ordinary shares) and YTTAW (warrants).

Yotta designs, builds and operates Tier III and Tier IV data centers in India, offering colocation, hyperscale and managed cloud services. The company is also positioning itself as an AI-compute provider. It has delivered a large share of the GPU capacity procured under the IndiaAI Mission, the government’s effort to expand access to local AI infrastructure.

In an exclusive interview with EE Times, Darshan Hiranandani, founder and chief executive officer of Nidar Infrastructure and Yotta Data Services, said the IndiaAI scheme has helped accelerate early demand, but the company sees broader drivers.

“Today about 60 percent of our capacity is given to India AI Mission and 40 percent is privately given. Next round it is a little skewed towards India 80–20, and in the third round we expect it to be in the other direction, 75 percent private sector,” Hiranandani said. “We would love to serve both markets and we will. The private sector market is not behind. It is just that the India AI Mission came, a large chunk of capital came, and we think it is a good mission.”

According to an investor presentation filed by Cartica, Yotta has 33 MW of live capacity and long-term development potential of up to 890 MW. Last year India’s National Informatics Centre hired Yotta to build and manage its data center in New Delhi as a part of the government’s ongoing mission to create sovereign cloud services over global hyperscalers.

Hiranandani said that hyperscalers “will never give you the option to say these servers are yours or where they sit,” whereas Yotta allows enterprise customers to retain control of their infrastructure while accessing AI workloads on demand.

Betting on NASDAQ over BSE

The documents filed with the U.S. SEC show rapid top-line growth. Revenue rose 123%, from $22 million in fiscal year 2023 to an estimated $49.2 million in fiscal year 2024. Net loss narrowed slightly from $53.2 million to $52.8 million over the same period. Yotta forecasts $156 million in revenue in fiscal year 2025, while projecting a deeper net loss of $113.4 million as it ramps up capital expenditure. The company expects to invest roughly $1 billion during the year.

Part of the capex surge comes from new sovereign and enterprise cloud commitments.

“Suddenly AI came on board, and we had to give a large chunk of three to four thousand million rupees,” Hiranandani said. “The next pipeline is another ten billion. That was when we realized we need to hit the markets to raise that kind of capital.”

Rather than a traditional initial public offering, the company opted for a SPAC merger.

“The merger with a SPAC is a more convenient way to arrive into the U.S. markets, from a regulation perspective. Our first objective was to get on and start trading so we can keep raising capital,” he said.

Hiranandani pushed back on comparisons with pandemic-era speculative SPACs.

“Most of those just had some concept. That is not our case today. We have hundreds of millions of dollars of revenue. Cartica is going to be a very small part of the merged entity, but an important part that allows us to go public,” he shared.

The company also expects practical advantages from a U.S. listing.

“The NASDAQ is very open to allow companies to constantly raise capital. When you are a listed company everything is disclosed, and you can get through diligence very quickly. The whole cycle moves fast. It also matters for many customers. You are dealing with the largest banks in the world. They feel happier that you have boards and audit committees because you are running systems important to them.”

On choosing New York over Mumbai, he said, “The AI story is well understood in the US markets. The Indian market is still figuring out what the AI story means. Today we do not have a domestic cloud provider of any scale that is listed. The NASDAQ understands the AI story. So that is our better bet.”

Prepping for a multi-gigawatt AI future

Yotta is one of Nvidia’s reference cloud partners, with the chipmaker buying capacity from the company’s facilities. Hiranandani said this reflects the firm’s approach to staying focused on infrastructure rather than building its own chips or models.

“We are happy being like TSMC, we do not want to be Nvidia. We want to be the platform where companies put up a model, get an API, and use it the way they want. Where we invest is in how to fine-tune, sharpen and improve that every day.”

Yotta is also exploring smaller sovereign-cloud deployments overseas.

“We can do it in a very small box,” he said, pointing to a cloud-layer agreement with Princess Noura University in Saudi Arabia, where Yotta will manage the software and operations stack while the university invests in hardware.

Hiranandani expects domestic cloud players to emerge overtime.

“Just like China has an Ali Cloud or Tencent Cloud, India will also have domestic players that will slowly grow and take the lead, and we will hopefully be one of them.”

He also believes international perception of India’s cloud ecosystem is changing.

“International investors are shocked about how little data centre capacity we have. It is only one point two gigawatts today. The U.S. is talking about sixty gigawatts. China is almost at seven or eight.”

Reflecting on his conversations with investors, Hiranandani revealed that the country’s purchasing power is uneven and cannot be judged by per-capita GDP alone.

“India has a one trillion-dollar economy and a three trillion-dollar economy. The one trillion-dollar economy can afford the same things as a Saudi Arabia economy. The three trillion-dollar economy, you have to serve through the government.”

Looking forward, he said consolidation in the sector is far off, but scale will matter.

“In the next one month, for the next 100,000 GPUs, we will probably be fastest to the market. Maybe even for the next 500,000 GPUs coming into India,” he said.

See also:

Vsora Tapes Out AI Inference Chip for Data Centers

Why Fiber Optics is Replacing Copper in Data Centers

Arm Begins 2nd Term on Nasdaq, at $54.5 Billion Valuation

First AI Chip Startup Goes Public as Blaize Merges With SPAC

data.title: Layoffs At Tenstorrent As Startup Pivots Towards Developer Sales
data.description:
//php echo do_shortcode('[responsivevoice_button voice="US English Male" buttontext="Listen to Post"]') ?>

AI chip startup Tenstorrent has undergone a round of layoffs, cutting 7.5% of its staff and bringing its headcount down to around 1,000.

While no particular part of the company is shrinking with layoffs coming from across the business, the company is nevertheless undergoing some changes, Tenstorrent CEO Jim Keller told EE Times.

“We grew really fast, and not everybody works out …. we’re not cutting for financial reasons,” Keller said. “It’s partly skills and mindset, partly team fit. Healthy organizations [lose] 6-10% of people voluntarily and involuntarily each year.”

The company reviews all new hires after two months, but it has become harder to identify employees who are not making the cut now that the company has shipped “hundreds” of servers, with manufacturing yield improving steadily, Keller said.

Changes in the business include increased use of AI for programming, which Keller said had been adopted more successfully by some team members, and less so by others.

Tenstorrent has also pivoted its sales strategy away from enterprise sales and towards individual developers, resulting in a reduction in size of the enterprise sales team.

“In the long run, the big guys will make [us] the most money, but that’s not where we are in our journey,” Keller said.

While the company is making enterprise sales, there is more to be learned by working directly with developers, he added.

“The number one rule of enterprise sales is never sell to little people, because it’s a waste of time, but we’re building a new product, a new technology,” Keller said. “A lot of really cool applications come directly from developers, so I’m telling the whole company that our focus is developers and small businesses, and we’ll work our way up.”

The exception is Tenstorrent’s IP business, which continues to sell to companies via its own sales team.

In terms of roadmap, Tenstorrent’s third-gen technology will be chiplet-based. These chiplets were due to tape out this quarter, but are now scheduled to tape out in Q1 2026. Keller ascribed delays to the acquisition of Blue Cheetah’s die-to-die PHY IP last summer, and the related publication of Tenstorrent’s Open Chiplet Architecture specification, both of which prompted design changes.

The company has undergone well-reported challenges with software development, but is steadily bringing up more models with around 70 up and running on BlackHole-generation hardware today, Keller said. A third party was able to bring up DeepSeek and Gemini models and while their performance was around halfway to Tenstorrent’s target, the companies are working together to find and fix bugs.

“The goal for this year is 100 models up and running [on BlackHole] and 1000 developers on Discord,” Keller said. “We’re hitting that.”

data.title: The Trillion-Dollar Race to Fragment the Nvidia Monopoly
data.description:
//php echo do_shortcode('[responsivevoice_button voice="US English Male" buttontext="Listen to Post"]') ?>

For the past ten years, Nvidia has dominated the market for advanced computer chips used in machine learning and artificial intelligence.

Driven by its proprietary CUDA software and rapid innovation, Nvidia became synonymous with AI processors, briefly reaching a $5 trillion market value this year. Between February and October 2025, Nvidia reported $147.8 billion in sales from chips, network connections, and related hardware supporting AI growth.

Even though Nvidia’s newest and most powerful processors, the Grace Blackwell series, are selling out quickly, its dominance is starting to weaken. Instead of facing just one competitor, Nvidia is now challenged on many fronts as the industry shifts toward more specialized hardware.

Nvidia’s strong sales and high profit margins are driven by scarcity, primarily due to manufacturing limits. The main bottleneck for high-end chips is the limited capacity for advanced Chip-on-Wafer-on-Substrate (CoWoS) packaging at TSMC.

Nvidia consumes most of this limited production, but competition for chip supply is intensifying. TSMC, the sole manufacturer, plans to expand capacity to 100,000 wafers per month by 2026. As supply constraints ease, companies like Google and AMD are expected to benefit.

Nvidia now faces significant risk as the industry shifts from experimenting with large foundation models to prioritizing large-scale, cost-effective inference.

Major cloud providers are moving away from reliance on Nvidia’s CUDA ecosystem. They are investing in their own specialized chips for high-volume inference, where operating costs now exceed training costs.

Nvidia’s largest customers become competitors

The “Big Four” North American hyperscalers—Google, Amazon Web Services, Microsoft, and Meta—account for the largest capital expenditures in this sector. Their collective shift to custom silicon is a strategic move to ensure competitiveness.

Google, part of Alphabet, initiated the move toward custom AI chips with its Tensor Processing Units (TPUs). The latest version, TPU v7 ‘Ironwood,’ is optimized for inference. Ironwood features a large shared memory, enabling up to 9,216 chips to connect in a single ‘superpod,’ which addresses memory constraints in large Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) models.

Meta Platforms may lease and potentially purchase Google’s TPU chips for its data centers starting in 2027. This would mark a shift for Google, positioning it as a merchant chip supplier. Some estimates suggest Google could capture up to 10% of Nvidia’s annual revenue, amounting to billions of dollars.

Amazon focuses on training and cost efficiency

Amazon Web Services (AWS) is pursuing improved price-performance to attract businesses seeking alternatives to Nvidia’s expensive chips. AWS claims its Trainium chips can reduce training costs by up to 50% compared to GPUs, targeting mid-range AI workloads.

AWS is also expanding its custom CPU, Graviton5, built on 3nm technology. It delivers up to 25% better performance than its predecessor for general tasks. Partners such as Anthropic use Trainium2 for model training, showcasing the value of AWS’s Trainium software and Neuron SDK.

Meta and Microsoft’s strategic shift

Meta adopts a specialized approach. Its Meta Training and Inference Accelerator (MTIA) is designed for high-volume tasks, such as powering recommendation systems for Facebook and Instagram. Offloading these tasks to custom chips allows Meta to reserve its Nvidia H100s for advanced AI research.

Microsoft’s custom silicon program has faced setbacks. The next-generation Maia chip, codenamed “Braga,” has been delayed until 2026, placing Microsoft in a challenging position. Due to this delay, Microsoft must continue purchasing Nvidia’s Blackwell GPUs at high prices to meet OpenAI’s computing demands. To offset costs, Microsoft is also using AMD’s MI300X GPUs, making it one of AMD’s largest customers.

Emerging competition and the Triton effect

As major cloud companies develop their own systems, Advanced Micro Devices (AMD) remains the primary alternative to Nvidia in the broader market. AMD’s MI300X chip features 192GB of HBM3 memory, significantly more than Nvidia’s H100, making it well-suited for large-model inference and reducing costs. AMD anticipates its data center GPU sales will reach billions annually, directly competing with Nvidia.

AMD’s previous software limitations are being addressed through OpenAI’s Triton compiler. Triton enables developers to write high-performance code compatible with both Nvidia and AMD hardware, without requiring deep expertise in CUDA or AMD’s ROCm.

Triton increases hardware interchangeability and simplifies the transition away from CUDA. This reduces costs and makes it easier for companies to adopt alternative suppliers.

China narrows the technology gap

Nvidia’s challenges are compounded by the emergence of a parallel, independent ecosystem in China, driven by U.S. export controls. Huawei leads this domestic infrastructure, compensating for limited access to EUV lithography with alternative architectural strategies.

Huawei’s leading chip, the Ascend 910C, is produced by SMIC using a 7nm Deep Ultraviolet (DUV) process and has demonstrated reliability. Reports indicate it achieves 60–80% of Nvidia’s H100 performance in training and matches it in some inference tasks.

This performance is enabled by a ‘scale-out’ design that connects thousands of Ascend NPUs in systems such as the Atlas 950 SuperPoD via a new optical link called UnifiedBus. This high-speed network compensates for lower manufacturing quality, allowing advanced model training.

However, developing this independent computing power is costly. The manufacturing yield for Ascend 910C chips at SMIC is only 30–40%, well below industry standards, but the Chinese government appears willing to accept these losses for strategic purposes.

HiSilicon, Huawei’s semiconductor company, plans to introduce new versions of the Ascent chips in 2026, namely the Ascend 950PR, which will launch in the first quarter of 2026, followed by a higher-memory variant, the 950DT, in the fourth quarter..

The paradox deepened this week when, despite U.S. President Trump signaling an easing of restrictions by approving the export of Nvidia’s advanced H200 chips, Beijing indicated it would impose its own stringent controls on access. Regulators overseeing China’s semiconductor independence campaign are considering ways to permit only limited access to the H200, Nvidia’s second-best generation of AI chip.

China is also advancing memory chip production. ChangXin Memory Technologies (CXMT) plans to begin mass-producing HBM3 chips by 2026, helping to mitigate future memory-related sanctions.

Investing in a fragmented market

Nvidia recognizes the challenge of maintaining high profit margins, particularly due to TSMC’s production constraints. In response, Nvidia is investing in new markets, including telecom infrastructure companies such as Nokia.

Despite the challenges, Nvidia is expected to remain the leader in high-margin advanced model training through 2026. However, the broader market for high-volume inference will likely be dominated by custom chips from major cloud providers.

The next major competition will focus on chip connectivity. As electrical connections reach their limits, optical connections are being adopted in commercial products. Marvell’s acquisition of Celestial AI and companies like Lightmatter, which offers a 3D photonic wafer for faster chip connections, indicate that optical links will become standard in leading AI chips by 2026. This shift will position companies such as Broadcom and Marvell as key suppliers.

Nvidia’s current challenge is to remain profitable and retain market share in a rapidly evolving industry where hardware is increasingly specialized. The future of AI computing will be defined by a mix of specialized systems connected by advanced technology, rather than a single monopoly.

See also:

Nvidia Navigates an Era of Growth Amidst Mounting Challenges

How Open Compute Initiatives Are Influencing AI Hardware Design

Nvidia’s $1 Billion Bet on Nokia to Rewire Global Telecom

TSMC Price Hikes End the Era of Cheap Transistors

data.title: Teleoperation: How Will It Impact AVs?
data.description:
//php echo do_shortcode('[responsivevoice_button voice="US English Male" buttontext="Listen to Post"]') ?>

Teleoperation is becoming a key component of autonomous vehicle deployment, enabling a range of emerging use cases. One segment in particular, remote driving, shows strong market potential across multiple industries, especially in consumer mobility. This article examines teleoperation functions, service segments, and real-world applications. It includes a profile of the German company Vay Technology to illustrate the growing consumer market opportunity for remote driving.

Overview

Teleoperation is defined as the remote control of a device across multiple industries, such as consumer, industrial, and military devices. This article focuses exclusively on vehicles. There are two main segments, with different remote capabilities: remote assistance (RA) and remote driving (RD). Table 1 provides details on the two segments.

RA has limited capabilities and is used as a backup to the software or virtual driver in an AV. AV service companies use some form of RA. Virtual drivers use RA information to resolve edge cases or unknown driving and traffic situations.

In RD, the vehicle is controlled remotely via a communication link and possibly a second, backup communication link. A remote driver controls all driving actions, with access to steering wheel and speed controls.

RA has three subsegments with increasing capabilities, as shown in Table 1. The segments are based on the teleoperation taxonomy defined by DriveU, an Israeli private automotive service platform.

The lowest capability is remote vehicle (RV) supervision, where RA monitors the RV and initiates a safe stop when needed. Advising an RV is the next capability; here, RA responds to RV queries such as edge cases. Guiding an RV is the highest capability; in this category, RA provides actions such as path planning.

RA is a key technology for all AV use cases. Most of the AV service providers have their own RA systems for use as a backup when an AV software driver experiences problems.

RD also has three subsegments with increasing capabilities, as summarized in Table 1. DriveU includes these subsegments in its teleoperation taxonomy (Figure 1).

The lowest RD capability is high-level control, such as lane and speed control. The RV manages all other controls and safety as well as minimal-risk maneuvers (MRM).

RD with low-level control manages trajectory and speed. The RV validates RD instructions and is in charge of safety and MRM.

RD direct drive is the highest control level; here, the remote driver is in charge of all safety functions. The only exception would be a loss of the communication link to the RD; in this case, the RV would stop and perform an MRM.

RA and RD use cases

There are numerous use cases for RA and RD, as summarized in Table 2. The first row lists key AV use cases, from robotaxis and robotrucks to delivery and fixed-route AVs. They all use RA technology to resolve edge cases and other unknown traffic situations.

Consumer use cases are primarily based on RD technology and are just emerging. Rental car delivery is the leading application; Vay is the front-runner in this market. Vehicle maintenance is another potential RD application; here, a remote driver pilots the car to and from a car dealer or other maintenance supplier. Refueling or EV charging is a similar RD application.

Other RD use cases include remote chauffeuring and valet parking.

Last-mile delivery AVs use both RD and RA. Sidewalk delivery AVs move at low speeds; some companies in this space are using RD, while others use AVs with accompanying RA.

There are numerous industrial uses of RD and some RA deployments for AV applications. Remote driving of warehouse forklifts got attention during the Covid pandemic and became a growing use case. Manufacturing industries have invested in automation for decades and are using RD for material handling and factory vehicles. Yard vehicles may be AVs with RA as a backup, or they may use RD.

Agricultural vehicles are using both RD and AV-RA; all major equipment makers have deployed thousands of such vehicles for functions such as seeding and harvesting.

The mining industry was an early user of RD and AV-RA, with the first such deployments dating back two decades. Mining is a risky environment, posing a higher risk of fatalities than most industries, which made RD and AVs an attractive investment. The mining industry uses thousands of self-driving vehicles.

Military vehicles likewise operate in risky environments, spurring investment in RD and AV-RA vehicles for defense applications. Applications include logistics, surveillance, reconnaissance, and many others.

Teleoperation companies

Few companies specialize in teleoperation; the total number of participants in the segment looks much larger when AV platform companies are included in the count, as most have developed proprietary RA software for their own use. Table 3 offers an overview of teleoperation companies. The focus is on RD-centric companies, but the top row provides some data on companies involved in RA for AVs.

Most robotaxi companies have RA capabilities as part of their AV software platforms. For other AV use cases, companies may have proprietary platforms or use teleoperation suppliers. Kodiak has an agreement to use Vay RD technology in addition to its own RA technology. A few goods AV companies use RD technology for sidewalk deliveries.

U.S.-based Designated Driver targets on-demand teleoperation for AVs. It can provide real-time teleoperation to any vehicle via hardware, software, remote driver station systems, and operations service. Designated Driver is positioning itself as a supplier of teleoperation as a service. It is a small company, with about 10 employees.

DriveU specializes in teleoperation, with important contributions to technology segmentation and successful deployments. Founded in Israel in 2019, DriveU has more than 30 employees and provides its Teleoperation Connectivity Platform to multiple industries and use cases, including robots, slow-moving vehicles, trucks, shuttles, and more. Serve Robotics, a sidewalk goods delivery company, uses DriveU’s Connectivity Platform.

Fernride is a German company, based in Munich, with a focus on automation for industrial trucks in multiple use cases, such as container terminals, yard trucks, and defense logistics. TÜV SÜD has certified Fernride’s safety and system design. Founded in 2019 after a decade of research at Technical University Munich, Fernride has received more than €75 million in VC funding and has more than 150 employees.

U.S.-based Guident focuses on a remote monitor and control center platform for AV fleet operators and robots. Its AV market position is to provide remote monitoring and assistance services or teleoperation as a service. It is a small company, with roughly 20 employees.

Ottopia uses a hybrid AI-powered teleoperation solution to control AVs remotely. The Israeli company provides a software platform that allows the human operator and the car’s AI to work together during a remote intervention. The platform is helpful in remote assistance, low-speed remote driving, monitoring performance, and similar actions. It serves industries including logistics, mining, agriculture, and military applications.

Teleo specializes in teleoperation for heavy equipment in off-road and potentially dangerous industries such as mining, agriculture, and construction. The U.S. company uses a ruggedized aftermarket kit and site mesh network that can work in remote areas with limited telecom services. Teleo is a small company, with about 20 employees.

Two teleoperation companies no longer exist: Phantom Auto and Voyage. Phantom Auto was an early entrant and raised nearly $100 million in VC funding but closed in March 2024. Voyage was acquired by Cruise in 2021, and General Motors acquired Cruise in 2016. With Cruise focusing on AV technology for GM’s vehicles, Voyage’s Telessist platform will likely be used in future GM AV products.

Vay Technology: business model and overview

Vay Technology launched RD services in Germany in early 2023 and bills itself as the first AV operation in Europe to offer services without a safety driver. Vay received an exemption permit from the German Authority for Traffic and Mobility Transition in December 2022, and its platform can drive without a safety driver on some public roads. An endorsement by TÜV SÜD was the prerequisite for the exemption permit. Vay’s main use case is remote driving to deliver and pick up rental cars.

Since 2021, Vay has been testing remotely operated electric cars on public roads in Berlin and Hamburg. Vay’s technology enables RD by professionally trained remote drivers operating from an RD station, equipped with a steering wheel, pedals, and other vehicle controls. The remote control system is developed according to automotive industry standards. The teledriver is in full control of the vehicle. The remote driver operates the vehicle without a safety driver and drives on a predefined route.

Since February 2023, with just a few taps in the Vay app, a remote driver can deliver an EV to a customer in Hamburg. The customer would then take control of the vehicle’s operation to drive to the destination. Upon arrival, the customer would simply step out, and a remote driver would park the car or drive it to the next customer. This service is offered at about half the cost of ride-hailing services. Vay positions its offering as the most affordable door-to-door mobility service and believes it can compete with private car ownership in urban areas.

Table 4 is an overview of Vay’s activities in Europe and the U.S. The company was founded in 2018 in Berlin and has raised more than $150 million in VC funding. It had approximately 180 employees as of mid-2025.

Vay’s main use case is delivery of vehicles to renters who drive themselves to their destination, with remote retrieval of the vehicles at the end of the rental. Remote drivers work in teledriving centers with 5G connections to the rented vehicles. The company operates in Germany and the U.S.

The cost of equipping a vehicle with teledriving capabilities is only a few thousand dollars, and the technology can be integrated into any model. Germany passed legislation enabling RD in July 2025, set to take effect in December 2025.

Vay currently operates RD services in Hamburg and Berlin on a limited basis. In February 2025, Vay started rental deliveries at Belgium’s Port of Antwerp-Bruges in partnership with Ush and Poppy Mobility.

Strong growth is forecast in Germany as the country’s RD regulations take effect in December 2025. Additional European countries are expected to adopt RD legislation in 2026 and beyond.

Las Vegas is currently Vay’s primary city for remote driving of rental cars. Operations started in January 2024 and expanded in June of the same year. In January 2025, Vay opened an 8,500-square-foot production facility in Las Vegas to integrate its RD systems into its Kia e-Niro EVs. Vay had 30 rental cars in operation in February 2025 and plans for 100 by the end of the year. Vay’s main RD center for cars is located in Las Vegas; it also established an RD operations center in the same city for trucks in February 2025.

By July 2025, Vay had completed more than 17,000 cumulative trips in Las Vegas, compared with 1,000 trips by June 2024 and 6,000 by January 2025—an increase of roughly 16,000 rides within a year, averaging over 1,300 trips per month. Average monthly trips for the first half of 2025 are estimated at more than 1,800. As Vay expands its fleet of RD rental cars in Las Vegas, cumulative customer trips are expected to reach up to 45,000 trips by the end of 2025.

Vay has multiple teleoperation use cases; Table 4 lists four of them, along with their potential advantages. RD for car rental delivery and pickup is currently the main focus, but remote truck driving is becoming increasingly significant.

Vay has a long-standing collaboration with Nvidia and advertises the partnership on its RD cars.

Kodiak Robotics uses Vay technology to supplement its fully autonomous operations with remote human assistance when needed. The Vay RD capability supports Kodiak’s Assisted Autonomy, a key feature of the Kodiak Driver autonomous solution for Kodiak trucks operating in the Permian Basin of West Texas and Eastern New Mexico. The Kodiak Driver uses Vay’s technology to support Assisted Autonomy, used by long-haul customers for launching and landing trucks at customer facilities and in other low-speed driving scenarios, including interpreting law enforcement hand signals.

Vay and Peugeot are exploring use cases for last-mile delivery, assessing the technology with a focus on B2B customers in both passenger vehicles and light commercial vehicles. Vay and Peugeot equipped a Peugeot E-308 passenger vehicle with RD technology and showed the car at the VivaTech 2024 conference in Paris.

The RD service Vay offers in Belgium is a partnership with Ush and Poppy Mobility, Belgium’s leading car-sharing and rental operator. The service delivers rental cars around the Port of Antwerp-Bruges.

In the public transportation space, Vay has partnered with Ioki, an independent subsidiary of Deutsche Bahn that is focused on AV technology for on-demand public transport. Ioki has multiple L4 testing projects with safety drivers in Europe. These include flagship projects such as Ioki Hamburg and Germany’s first self-driving public bus.

Vay has done 18 demonstrations for B2B partners, exploring a variety of use cases. They include RD integration into privately owned vehicles and applications for trucks and AVs. Vay believes these discussions show the versatility of remote driving and its potential to address diverse industry needs.

Summary

Teleoperation is emerging both as a companion to AVs and as a substitute for them. Teleoperation startups have seen limited success so far, however, and several have closed down. Most current companies are small, but some have received considerable VC funding.

Teleoperation has two segments based on capabilities for remote driving and for the level of remote control actions. Each segment has three subsegments based on overall capabilities. Figure 2 shows the teleoperation features of the RD and RA segments.

The RA segments (red boxes in the figure) have lower capabilities than the RD segments (green). In both cases, the RV receives attention from a remote operator. Services range from safety monitoring to complete remote driving.

At the lowest RA level, the RV does all the driving, with remote monitoring providing a safe stop only if needed. All safety and driving functions are controlled by the RV. At the highest RD level, the remote driver controls all the driving functions, including safety functions.

The teleoperation companies are a mixed bag of small companies focusing on different industries that have different characteristics and needs. AV platform companies such as Waymo and Aurora have their own RA technologies as part of their software driver platforms. The RD startups are trying to forecast and enter industries where RD provides value, efficiencies, cost savings, and added safety for on-board human drivers.

Vay has secured more than $150 million, the highest level of VC investment, and employs by far the largest workforce in the sector. The reason for this leading status is that Vay is focusing on a new autonomous market with low add-on system cost compared with AV use cases, which require high sensor counts and incur high computing costs. Vay’s RD market focuses on everyday tasks that simplify many routine driving trips. Examples include rental car pickup and dropoff, trips to and from car maintenance appointments, and trips for car refueling/EV charging. These use cases are easy to understand for potential customers.

Adding the hardware and software for remote driving costs a few thousand dollars and can be done with aftermarket systems. Transmission costs for RD communication links will add some costs depending on location. These costs are currently a fraction of AV system costs and will remain lower even as AV costs decline by a factor of 5× in the next decade.

Vay’s deployment in Las Vegas demonstrates the market opportunity. Vay was doing nearly 2,000 rental car trips per month in Las Vegas in the first half of 2025 with about 30 vehicles but expects to increase to 100 RD vehicles in Las Vegas by the end of the year. Vay’s growth in Las Vegas based on cumulative rides could grow by a factor of 3× to 4× by early 2026.

With new legislation allowing remote driving in Germany starting December 2025, Vay is set to deploy its RD platform rapidly in that country. Other EU countries are likely to add similar RD legislation in 2026.

RD is also expanding across multiple industry verticals, including mining, agriculture, defense, logistics, and manufacturing.

data.title: India Paves Dual RISC-V Tracks with C-DAC and Startup Silicon
data.description:
//php echo do_shortcode('[responsivevoice_button voice="US English Male" buttontext="Listen to Post"]') ?>

A global move toward customizable processor architectures is creating room for India to push into RISC-V, with government labs and young chip firms taking different paths. India is now coupling a state-funded processor effort with a parallel rise of startups using RISC-V for embedded and IoT designs.

The global RISC-V market is no longer a niche experiment. The RISC-V Market Analysis 2024 report by the SHD group forecasted that this market will climb to $2.95 billion by 2028, growing at almost 30% every year. Most of this momentum is coming from the embedded and IoT side, where engineers want more control over the microarchitecture and fewer licensing hurdles. Asia-Pacific is leading that shift, with China driving the volume and countries like India using RISC-V to build custom silicon without paying for proprietary cores.

Against this backdrop, India’s state-backed push through the Centre for Development of Advanced Computing’s (C-DAC) Vega and Tejas processors, and a commercial track led by startups backed by India’s design-linked incentive scheme and Chips-to-Startups program, reveal how India is trying to place itself inside a fast-expanding global market rather than build in isolation.

India’s commercial track: finding opportunity in the gaps

For companies like Mindgrove Technologies, the path into RISC-V is not driven by ideology or by trying to out-engineer the behemoths. It is shaped by a simple commercial reality that the embedded market is far broader than any single vendor can serve. “We found that although there are a large number of players, the market opportunity is larger than anybody is able to fulfil on their own,” Mindgrove Technologies co-founder T. R. Shashwath told EE Times.

RISC-V itself enters the picture only where it is useful. “Nobody buys a RISC-V chip or an Arm chip because it is RISC-V or Arm,” Shashwath said. In Mindgrove’s markets—mid-range embedded and IoT—it is already happening quietly. ESP32-class devices have begun shifting, toolchains are compatible and the open microarchitecture allows the company to adjust the core pipeline when customers need specific latency fixes. “That is a strength,” he added.

Shashwath explained that his approach has been to identify the “last-mile ten per cent” of requirements that differ from customer-to-customer and compress them into a solvable design problem. The goal, he said, is to find what is common rather than chase bespoke requirements that customers will not pay for. “Ultimately, volume makes the difference,” he said. “My job as a designer is to find a way to bridge the gap between all of these last-mile ten per cent …. and then it becomes a viable option because I am amortizing the cost over several players.”

Rather than position itself against its established competitors, the startup leans into the idea that differentiation is no longer about counting cores or clock speeds. “You cannot really differentiate in terms of somebody having three and somebody having five,” Shashwath said.

Instead, the company focuses on how customers interact with the chip, through development tools, reference hardware and the support ecosystem around it. “An integrated development environment is everything,” he said, adding that Mindgrove is building its own modules and working with partners so customers “can begin using the product with minimal overhead.”

That ecosystem strategy ties directly to survival. Shashwath said long-term viability comes from targeting niches the larger players do not touch and serving sectors with long qualification cycles. Once an industrial customer qualifies a part, they tend not to switch for a decade or more. Beyond that, he acknowledged the uncertain nature of the industry. “There is a lot of luck involved,” he said. “We are building a lot of fundamental technology so that we are nimble and agile enough to respond to changes as they are happening in real time.”

Resistance to an Indian-built RISC-V chip, he added, has eased over time. Government endorsement helped, but the bigger shift came from positioning Mindgrove not as a chip vendor but as a problem solver. “If they are looking for a change because of cost or a feature that they are not able to get, then the resistance naturally drops.”

Its first wave of products, including the Secure IoT device, is now nearing production after what Shashwath described as “many interesting twists and turns.” A second family, Vision, is entering internal prototyping, with sample availability expected next year. Mindgrove has also pre-tested its modules against India’s Standardization Testing and Quality Certification regime, which requires compliance across 36 criteria. “We meet all thirty-six,” he said, adding that Secure IoT already “meets thirty-five out of thirty-six.”

The state track: C-DAC’s bid to build a national baseline

Running parallel to the startup activity is India’s state-backed effort, anchored in C-DAC’s Vega and Tejas processors. Unlike commercial vendors chasing narrow segments, C-DAC’s mandate is broader—build a family of open, licensable RISC-V processors that academia and industry can adopt without depending on foreign IP.

“Vega is part of the Digital India RISC-V program funded by the Ministry of Electronics and IT,” said Libin T. T., scientist F at C-DAC. The series ranges “from the microcontroller class to high-performance embedded class processors,” with some already taped out as ASICs.

Tejas 32, the first ASIC, now ships inside C-DAC’s Aries development boards. Arduino compatibility was intentional, he said, because it allows universities and smaller manufacturers to plug it easily into existing development workflows.

Despite a crowded field of global RISC-V vendors, C-DAC argues that the Vega designs hold their own. “When you compare the CoreMark and Dhrystone benchmark, the Vega processors perform at par with other IPs available in the market,” he said.

Adoption, however, has followed a familiar trajectory—initial hesitation, slow awareness-building and gradual uptake as RISC-V gained legitimacy worldwide. “When we introduced the Vega processors and development boards in 2022, there was some hesitation,” Libin said. That has changed as developers have become more familiar with the ISA and as C-DAC expanded its outreach. “Many in academia and industry are purchasing our processor IPs, and some are buying the Tejas 32 SoC for integration into their product designs.”

To bridge the gap between strong technical R&D and commercial traction, C-DAC has been developing reference designs and fabricating multiple SoCs using different versions of the Vega core. Several new boards and SoCs will be available in the coming months, giving customers hands-on access. Some partners are working with Vega for networking applications, while others are exploring IoT.

C-DAC is also exploring different licensing models, including open-source options. Libin said the organization is evaluating both commercial and open licensing paths to widen adoption, keeping performance transparency as a guiding principle. Benchmark data is published openly, which he said helps maintain quality while allowing decentralized development in states like Kerala.

Much of this effort is tied to Kerala’s engineering ecosystem, which plays an outsized role in India’s open-source hardware community. C-DAC’s teams draw heavily from local talent, and Libin credited “the commitment and hard work” of the region’s engineers for sustaining the program.

Where the tracks converge

Together, the government and private sector tracks show India testing two different theories of how a country enters a global architecture shift. C-DAC is building a baseline: processors, boards, IP blocks and a licensing model that fits public-sector needs. Meanwhile, startups like Mindgrove are navigating the market from the other direction, looking for underserved segments, long qualification cycles and small advantages that come from being closer to the engineering problem.

Both sides agree on one point that is shaping RISC-V adoption globally: customers do not migrate because the ISA is open or fashionable. They move when it solves a real problem. India’s RISC-V story, so far, is unfolding along that line.

See also:

India Riding Wave of Confidence With Chip Design Ecosystem

RISC-V Thrives Through Research, International Collaboration

data.title: Nobel Winner’s Quantum Startup Forms Industry Alliance
data.description:
//php echo do_shortcode('[responsivevoice_button voice="US English Male" buttontext="Listen to Post"]') ?>

Nobel Physics prizewinner John Martinis has positioned his startup, Qolab, at the top of a quantum-computing alliance with chip industry leaders like Applied Materials and Synopsys, aiming to revamp how quantum devices are made.

Martinis told EE Times in an exclusive interview that the newly formed Quantum Scaling Alliance (QSA) expects to make the world’s first quantum devices with more than a million qubits ahead of larger companies like Google and Amazon by reducing the “forest of wires” in current designs to tiny metal traces on a silicon wafer.

On Nov. 10, Applied Materials, HPE, Synopsys and Qolab announced they are among eight members in the QSA, a global group aiming to make quantum computing scalable, practical and transformative across industries. Masoud Mohseni from HPE Labs, the applied research arm of HPE, will oversee the initiative and serve as quantum system architect. The Alliance is co-led by Martinis, a 2025 Nobel Laureate recognized for his pioneering advances in quantum computing who is also co-founder and CTO at Qolab.

“If we want to make a million-qubit device, there’s a scaling and wiring issue with superconducting qubits,” Martinis told EE Times. “I think the approach everyone’s taking right now is okay, but it’s going to hit a box canyon at some point. It’ll be limited. We’ve been proposing a way to use modern semiconductor fabrication to get around that and to improve things.”

In 2022, Martinis co-founded Qolab in Los Angeles, Calif.

Martinis recalls a few years before when he was designing quantum processors at Google.

“When I worked at Google, they were trying to do everything themselves, and that’s one model. The problem is if you want to have the best manufacturing, someone in the semiconductor world wants to go off to this weird quantum project for a couple of years, and then if it doesn’t work out, well, that’s maybe not so good for their career.”

Martinis wants to work with experts.

“Getting the best expertise to solve these problems is a more efficient way. But it means you have to collaborate and work together, and the lawyers have to work together to sign the IP agreements. It’s not easy.”

Forest of wires

Martinis is focused on reducing a forest of wires in today’s quantum devices to tiny metal traces on a silicon wafer.

“Instead of using co-ax wires, which people are talking about now, we’re using a silicon wafer. The nice thing about a 300-millimeter wafer is you can make half-micron, micron-wide wires and have a hundred thousand wires going from low temperature to high temperature. That way, a very complex, bulky system can be made on a wafer. Then you connect that wafer to your qubits at low temperature. We have an idea how to integrate that.”

Qolab is working with QSA partner Applied Materials to do the fabrication of the quantum devices. Hewlett Packard spinoff HPE will handle what Martinis called the “high end” of the manufacturing stack, combining classical supercomputers with quantum computers.

“We’re experts on the quantum design part. We work with Applied Materials, which does the fabrication,” he said. “We’ll work with Synopsys to develop the tools. For example, we’re going to need a process design kit for the fabrication. They have expertise in that. We’re going to need to have simulation tools, which is something that they do.”

Quantum alliance

The QSA is based on a collaboration between member companies that have been working together for about a year, Synopsys Distinguished Architect Igor Markov told EE Times. More members are welcome, he said.

“The idea is to develop an ecosystem where different members, small companies, large companies, and even academics, contribute to different aspects of quantum computing, which we view as a very vertical enterprise, spanning topics from atomic simulation to supercomputing systems,” Markov said.

Synopsys tools model everything from atoms to systems, Markov noted.

“We literally are working with atoms here, and we’re going all the way up to systems,” he said of the QSA.

The QSA believes that one of the best ways to advance quantum computing is to reuse the huge investments made in the semiconductor fabrication and design ecosystems. Synopsys represents a significant part of that ecosystem, Markov noted.

The main technology under development in the QSA now is superconducting qubits, the tech pioneered by Qolab and Martinis, who won the Nobel prize based on his supercomputing work, according to Markov.

From the Synopsys perspective, a couple of other technologies besides supercomputing qubits are under consideration, Markov said. Synopsys is also evaluating single-electron spin tech for quantum computing because it uses traditional metal-oxide-semiconductor processes, while research and industry efforts have emphasized the use of CMOS-compatible processes for scalability.

“Synopsys’ mission is to help with engineering tools,” Markov said. “If they’re quantum chips, if they’re non-quantum chips, we can help.”

Synopsys, especially with its recent acquisition of Ansys, has a “broad array” of tools for multi-physics simulation, Markov noted.

“For some technology that is non-silicon, we may be able to help. Whereas with the QSA, the emphasis is really on semiconductors and related technologies.”

QSA’s vision sets 2033 as a milestone when quantum computers scale to as many as five million physical qubits. With quantum error correction, physical qubits support a smaller number of logical qubits—up to a hundred times fewer—that “really work” and support algorithms, Markov said.

“We are looking at qubit devices that implement individual qubits and then of course interconnect, scaling them to the chip level and to the wafer-integration level,” he added. “This is somewhat similar to what Cerebras is doing, but of course they’re not doing quantum.”

Synopsys notes that among quantum-computing companies and university researchers, many are using “homegrown” or open-source simulation tools.

Quantum effects are very hard to simulate, Markov noted.

“These tools are limited and even when they’re state-of-the-art for some particular function, the integration of multiple methods and the combination of different numerical techniques only appears in in commercially supported software,” Markov said.

When Martinis was with Google around the beginning of 2020, the company reached 53 qubits.

“They were talking at the time by the end of the decade to get to a million qubits,” Martinis said. “It’s halfway through the decade, and they’re at 100 qubits. If you extrapolate out at that rate, I will certainly be dead by the time they build a general-purpose quantum computer, which is unacceptable for me.”

China is another reason why Martinis feels pressed to accelerate development. He warned that China is “nanoseconds” behind the U.S. in a quantum-computing race, according to a Bloomberg article.

He declined to go into detail on how Qolab is able to run circuits on a silicon wafer under the wide temperature extremes of a quantum computer.

“The field is very competitive, and I’m very concerned about China stealing our things. We’ve talked to various companies in the U.S., and they’re waiting to see if we can get it to work,” he told EE Times. “I’m actually much more concerned about China because they will take our ideas and run with them right away.”
